heroHeadline: Senate Votes 50-48 to Advance $70B ICE/CBP Funding via Reconciliation; DHS Shut Down Since Feb 14 After Minneapolis Shootings
heroSubtitle: The U.S. Senate voted 50-48 in the predawn hours of April 23, 2026, to adopt a budget resolution authorizing up to $70 billion in new ICE and Border Patrol funding through budget reconciliation — bypassing Democratic filibuster demands for use-of-force reforms triggered by the January 2026 fatal shootings of two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis by federal agents (Renée Good and Alex Pretti). Only Sens. Rand Paul (R-KY) and Lisa Murkowski (R-AK) defected; the measure now heads to the House with a Trump-imposed June 1 deadline. ICE remains in partial DHS shutdown mode since February 14. Separately, a KCUR/Beacon investigation confirmed Kansas City International Airport as a major ICE Air deportation hub (130 flights in 2025; up 200%). FY2026 custody deaths stand at 29 — a 22-year record; acting ICE Director Todd Lyons departs May 31.
footerNote: Data sourced from DHS, ICE official records, ACLU, HRW, DHS OIG, TRAC Reports, Detention Watch Network, court records, and Congressional records
